1.1: From Paul, appointed an apostle of Christ Jesus by God's own will, and from Sosthenes, our fellow believer.
1.2: To God's church in Corinth—to all who have been set apart in Christ Jesus and called as his holy people—and to everyone everywhere who calls on the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, who is their Lord and ours.
1.3: May God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ grant you grace and peace.
1.4: I give thanks to God constantly for you, because of the grace God poured out on you through Christ Jesus.
1.5: Through your union with Christ you have been made rich in every way: in every kind of speech and in all depth of understanding.
1.6: This confirms that what we testified about Christ is true in your experience.
1.7: As a result you have received every spiritual gift and lack nothing while you wait for the return of our Lord Jesus Christ.
1.8: He will keep you strong right up to the end so that you stand blameless on the day when our Lord Jesus Christ is revealed.
1.9: God keeps every promise he makes—and it was he who called you into this partnership with his Son, Jesus Christ our Lord.
1.10: I urge you, brothers and sisters, by the authority of our Lord Jesus Christ—stop the quarreling! Let there be no factions among you. Be knit together in perfect harmony, united in your thinking and your purpose.
1.11: Some of Chloe's people have told me about your disputes, dear brothers and sisters, and I must address it directly.
1.12: Each of you is taking sides: 'I am a follower of Paul,' or 'I follow Apollos,' or 'I follow Peter,' or 'I follow only Christ.'
1.13: Has Christ been split into factions? Was it Paul who was crucified for you? Were you baptized in Paul's name? Of course not!
1.14: I am thankful that I baptized none of you except Crispus and Gaius,
1.15: for now no one can claim they were baptized in my name.
1.16: Oh yes—I also baptized the household of Stephanas. I honestly cannot recall baptizing anyone else.
1.17: Christ did not send me to perform baptisms, but to proclaim the good news—and not with clever rhetoric, for that would empty the cross of Christ of its power.
1.18: The message about the cross sounds like sheer nonsense to those who are headed for destruction. But to us who are being saved, it is nothing less than the power of God at work.
1.19: As Scripture says, 'I will destroy the wisdom of the wise and make the intelligence of the intelligent utterly pointless.'
1.20: So where does that leave the brilliant thinkers, the scholars, and the world's great debaters? God has made the wisdom of this world look utterly foolish.
1.21: Since God in his wisdom ensured that human wisdom could never bring the world to know him, he chose to use what sounds like a foolish message to save those who trust in him.
1.22: The Jews demand spectacular signs, and the Greeks go looking for philosophical wisdom,
1.23: but we proclaim Christ crucified—a scandal to the Jews and sheer nonsense to the Gentiles.
1.24: Yet to those who have been called by God—Jews and Gentiles alike—Christ himself is the power of God and the wisdom of God.
1.25: What God does that looks foolish to us is wiser than the greatest human wisdom, and what looks weak in God is stronger than the mightiest human force.
1.26: Think about your own calling, brothers and sisters. By the world's standards, not many of you were wise, not many were powerful, and not many came from prominent families.
1.27: But God deliberately chose what the world considers foolish in order to shame those who think themselves wise. He chose what the world considers powerless to shame those who are strong.
1.28: God chose those the world counts as nothing—the despised, the overlooked, the powerless—to bring to nothing what the world regards as something.
1.29: He did all this so that no human being could ever stand before God and claim credit for themselves.
1.30: God united you with Christ Jesus. Christ himself has become our wisdom—he has made us right with God, set us apart as holy, and freed us from sin.
1.31: Therefore, as Scripture says, 'If you want to boast, boast only about what the Lord has done.'

2.1: When I first came to you, brothers and sisters, I did not bring brilliant arguments or high-sounding philosophy. I simply proclaimed to you the truth about God.
2.2: I deliberately kept my message simple—nothing but Jesus Christ, and him crucified.
2.3: I arrived among you feeling weak and nervous, quite overwhelmed by the task.
2.4: My message and my preaching carried no clever argument or polished rhetoric—only the Spirit's own convincing power.
2.5: I wanted your faith to stand on God's power alone, not on any human reasoning.
2.6: There is a genuine wisdom we share with those who are spiritually mature—but it is not the wisdom of this present age, nor of this world's rulers, who are rapidly losing their grip on things.
2.7: We speak of God's secret wisdom—kept hidden through all the ages, yet planned by God before time itself began, destined to bring us into glory.
2.8: Not one of this world's rulers grasped this hidden wisdom. If they had, they would never have crucified the Lord of glory.
2.9: But as Scripture hints: 'No eye has seen it, no ear has heard it, no mind can fully conceive it—all that God has prepared for those who love him.'
2.10: Yet God has revealed these very things to us by his Spirit—for the Spirit probes all depths, even the deepest mysteries of God.
2.11: No one can know another person's innermost thoughts except that person's own spirit. In the same way, no one can know God's thoughts except God's own Spirit.
2.12: We have not received the spirit of this world—we have received the Spirit who comes from God himself, so that we can truly understand and receive everything God has so freely given us.
2.13: When we tell you these things, we do not use words invented by human wisdom. Instead, we use words the Spirit himself teaches us, interpreting spiritual realities in Spirit-given language.
2.14: But a person who lives apart from the Spirit cannot receive the gifts of God's Spirit—they seem like nonsense to such a person, and they cannot be understood without a spiritual mind to perceive them.
2.15: The person who is truly spiritual can evaluate all things rightly, yet no merely human standard of judgment can evaluate such a person.
2.16: For as Scripture asks, 'Who can know the Lord's thoughts well enough to advise him?' But we have been given the mind of Christ—we think and discern with his wisdom.

3.1: But I could not address you as people matured by the Spirit. You were still like infants in the faith, still controlled by your earthly nature.
3.2: I gave you spiritual milk, not solid teaching, because you were not mature enough for it. And you are still not ready,
3.3: because you are still controlled by your earthly nature. When you are jealous of one another and quarreling among yourselves, doesn't that prove it? You are living by purely human standards.
3.4: When one of you says, 'I am a follower of Paul,' and another says, 'I follow Apollos,' you are acting just like ordinary, un-spiritual people.
3.5: Who are Apollos and Paul anyway? We are merely God's servants who brought you to faith. Each of us did whatever task the Lord assigned.
3.6: I planted the seed, Apollos watered it—but it was God who made it grow.
3.7: It is not the planter or the waterer who matters—only God, who gives the growth.
3.8: The planter and the waterer work together as partners with a single goal, and each will be rewarded in proportion to their own work.
3.9: We are all God's partners in his work—and you are God's farm and God's house.
3.10: Because of the grace God gave me, I laid the foundation as a skilled master builder. Now others are building on it—but every builder must be careful how he builds.
3.11: For no one can lay any other foundation than the one that has already been laid: Jesus Christ himself.
3.12: Anyone who builds on that foundation may use a variety of materials—gold, silver, jewels, wood, hay, or straw.
3.13: But on the day of judgment, fire will reveal what kind of work each builder has done. That fire will test whether the work was worth keeping.
3.14: If the work survives the fire, that builder will be rewarded.
3.15: But if the work is burned up, the builder will suffer great loss. The builder will be saved—but like someone who barely escapes through a wall of flames.
3.16: Don't you realize that all of you together are the temple of God, and that God's Spirit lives in you?
3.17: God will destroy anyone who destroys this temple—for God's own temple is holy, and you are that temple.
3.18: Stop deceiving yourselves! If you think you are wise by this world's standards, you need to become a fool in the world's eyes before you can be truly wise.
3.19: For what the world calls wisdom is foolishness before God. As Scripture says, 'He traps the wise in the snare of their own cleverness;'
3.20: and again, 'The Lord sees through the reasoning of the wise—all their brilliant thoughts lead nowhere.'
3.21: So stop boasting about following particular human teachers. Everything belongs to you—
3.22: Paul, Apollos, Peter, the entire world, life itself, death, the present and the future—all of it is yours.
3.23: And you belong to Christ, and Christ belongs to God.

4.1: Think of us this way: we are servants of Christ, and we have been entrusted with the sacred things of God.
4.2: The one requirement for a steward is this: he must be found trustworthy.
4.3: Frankly, it matters very little to me whether you or any human tribunal passes judgment on me. I don't even pass judgment on myself.
4.4: My conscience is clear—but that doesn't settle the matter. The Lord is the one who evaluates me, and his judgment is the only one that counts.
4.5: So stop judging before the right time comes. Wait for the Lord to appear. He will drag into the light what is now hidden in the dark, and he will lay bare every hidden motive of every heart. Then—and only then—will God give each person the recognition they truly deserve.
4.6: Brothers and sisters, I have used Apollos and myself as illustrations for your sake. Through us I want you to learn what 'Do not go beyond what is written' means—so that none of you will take sides, boasting about one leader over another.
4.7: Who gave you any special standing above anyone else? Everything you have was given to you as a gift. If that is true, why are you boasting as though you earned it yourself?
4.8: You think you have it all already! You think you are already rich! You act as though the kingdom has arrived and you are already reigning—without us! I wish you really were reigning, so we could reign alongside you!
4.9: I sometimes think God has placed us apostles at the very end of the line—like prisoners of war paraded before the crowd just before they are executed. We have become a public spectacle before the entire universe: angels and humans alike are watching.
4.10: We are fools for the sake of Christ—but you are so wise in Christ! We are weak—but you are strong! You are held in honor—but we are looked down on!
4.11: Right up to this very hour we are hungry and thirsty. We wear rags. We are beaten. We have no place to call home.
4.12: We work hard with our own hands. When people curse us, we bless them. When they hunt us down, we keep going.
4.13: When people slander us, we speak kindly in return. We have become the world's filth—the dregs scraped off the bottom—and we are still treated that way.
4.14: I'm not writing all this to make you feel ashamed. I'm writing it because I love you, as a father urges his own children.
4.15: You might have ten thousand instructors in Christ, but you don't have many fathers. I am your father—I brought you to life in Christ Jesus through the gospel.
4.16: So I urge you: follow my example.
4.17: That is why I am sending Timothy to you. He is my beloved and trustworthy son in the Lord. He will remind you how I conduct myself in Christ Jesus—which is exactly what I teach in all the churches everywhere.
4.18: Some of you have grown arrogant, thinking I would never actually come to deal with it.
4.19: I will come to you soon—if the Lord allows. Then I will see for myself: not what these proud people are saying, but whether there is any real power behind it.
4.20: God's kingdom is not about impressive words—it is about real power.
4.21: What will it be? Do you want me to come with a stick in hand, or do you want me to come to you in love and with a gentle spirit? The choice is yours.

5.1: The word is out that there is sexual immorality in your community—of a kind that shocks even the pagan world. A man is actually living with his stepmother.
5.2: And you are proud of yourselves! You should be in mourning instead—and you should remove from your fellowship the man who did this.
5.3: Even though I am not there with you in body, I am there in spirit—and I have already rendered my verdict on this man, as if I were standing there in person.
5.4: When you gather in the name of our Lord Jesus, and my spirit is there with you, and the power of our Lord Jesus is at work—
5.5: hand this man over to Satan. The aim is the destruction of his sinful flesh, so that on the day of the Lord Jesus his spirit will be saved.
5.6: There is nothing good about your boasting here. Don't you know that a small amount of yeast spreads through the whole batch of dough?
5.7: Clear out the old yeast, so you can be the fresh, unleavened community you truly are. Christ, our Passover lamb, has already been slaughtered.
5.8: So let us celebrate the festival—but not with the old yeast of malice and evil. Let us celebrate with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.
5.9: In an earlier letter I told you not to associate with sexually immoral people—
5.10: I did not mean the immoral people of the wider world, or greedy people, or swindlers, or idol worshipers—because then you would have to leave the world altogether.
5.11: What I mean is this: do not associate with anyone who claims to be a fellow believer but is sexually immoral, greedy, an idol worshiper, an abusive talker, a drunk, or a swindler. Don't even sit down to eat with such a person.
5.12: It is not my business to judge those outside your community. You are responsible for judging those inside it.
5.13: God will deal with outsiders. Your task is clear: remove the wicked person from your midst.

6.1: When one of you has a complaint against another believer, do you actually dare to bring it before a pagan court rather than before God's people?
6.2: Don't you know that God's people will one day judge the world? If the world comes under your judgment, surely you are capable of settling minor disputes among yourselves.
6.3: Don't you know that we will one day judge angels? If that is true, how much more can we handle ordinary everyday disputes!
6.4: So when you have disputes about this-world matters, why are you placing them before judges who carry no weight in the church?
6.5: I say this to shame you. Can it really be true that there is not a single person among you wise enough to settle a dispute between fellow believers?
6.6: Instead, believers are dragging other believers into court before people who don't even believe in God.
6.7: The fact that you have lawsuits against one another at all is already your defeat. Why not accept the injustice? Why not let yourselves be cheated?
6.8: But instead you are the ones doing wrong and cheating—and doing it to your own brothers and sisters.
6.9: Don't you know that the unrighteous will have no share in God's kingdom? Don't be deceived about this: the sexually immoral, idol worshipers, adulterers, and those who engage in same-sex intercourse—whether as the passive or the active partner—
6.10: nor thieves, the greedy, drunkards, abusive people, or swindlers—none of them will inherit God's kingdom.
6.11: And some of you were exactly like that. But you were washed clean. You were set apart as holy. You were declared righteous—all in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God.
6.12: You say, 'I am free to do anything.' Yes—but not everything is good for you. You say, 'I am free to do anything.' Yes—but I refuse to let anything become my master.
6.13: You say, 'Food is made for the stomach and the stomach for food.' Perhaps—but God will one day bring both to nothing. What matters is this: your body was not made for sexual immorality. It was made for the Lord, and the Lord cares deeply about your body.
6.14: God raised the Lord from the dead—and he will raise us too by that same power.
6.15: Don't you know that your bodies are actually parts of Christ himself? Should I then take what belongs to Christ and join it to a prostitute? God forbid!
6.16: Don't you know that when a man joins himself to a prostitute he becomes physically one with her? Scripture says, 'The two shall become one flesh.'
6.17: But the one who is joined to the Lord becomes one spirit with him.
6.18: Run from sexual immorality. Every other sin a person commits is external to the body—but sexual immorality is a sin against your own body.
6.19: Don't you know that your body is a temple where the Holy Spirit lives—the Spirit you received from God? You do not belong to yourself.
6.20: You were purchased at a price. So use your body to bring glory to God.

13.1: Though I could speak every human language and the language of angels, if I have no love, I am nothing more than a noise—a hollow gong, a jangling cymbal.
13.2: And if I had the power of prophecy and knew every hidden mystery, possessing all knowledge—even a faith that could move mountains—without love I would be nothing at all.
13.3: And if I distributed every last thing I owned to feed the hungry, and gave up my body to be burned—without love, it would gain me nothing.
13.4: Love holds on without giving up, and pours itself out in kindness. Love is not eaten up with envy. It does not strut or swagger. It is not full of itself.
13.5: Love does not act shamefully toward others. It does not insist on its own way. It does not flare up in anger. It does not file away a list of wrongs received.
13.6: Love takes no pleasure in evil; it celebrates only what is true.
13.7: Love bears every burden, puts its full trust in God, holds on to hope in every circumstance, and stands firm through every storm.
13.8: Love will never come to an end. Prophecies will run their course and fall silent. Tongues will cease. Knowledge will be left behind. But love goes on.
13.9: Our knowledge is partial, and our prophesying is partial—
13.10: —but when what is whole and complete arrives, everything partial will be left behind.
13.11: When I was a child, I spoke like a child, thought like a child, and reasoned like a child. When I grew up, I left all those childish ways behind. One stage of life gives way to the next.
13.12: Right now we see only a dim, puzzling reflection—as if peering into a polished bronze mirror. But one day we will see face to face. Now I know only part of the truth; then I will know fully, just as God has always fully known me.
13.13: Three things endure: faith, hope, and love. And the greatest of all three is love.

14.1: Make love your highest pursuit, and then be zealous for the gifts of the Spirit—above all, the gift of prophecy.
14.2: When someone speaks in a tongue, they are not addressing other people—they are speaking directly to God. No one can follow what they say, because by the Spirit they are speaking hidden things.
14.3: But the one who prophesies speaks directly to people, building them up, urging them forward, and strengthening their hearts.
14.4: Speaking in a tongue builds up the individual; prophesying builds up the whole congregation.
14.5: I would be glad for every one of you to speak in tongues—but I would rather you prophesied. Unless someone interprets so that the congregation is built up, the prophet holds the greater gift.
14.6: Think about it, brothers and sisters: if I came to you and only spoke in tongues, what benefit would that be? I would only be helping you if I brought something you could actually receive—a revelation, a piece of knowledge, a prophetic word, or some teaching.
14.7: Even inanimate instruments—a flute, a harp—have to produce distinct notes. Otherwise, how can anyone tell what melody is being played?
14.8: If a battle trumpet sounds an unclear note, no soldier will know to arm himself.
14.9: The same applies when you speak: unless the words you use can be understood, you might as well be talking to the wind.
14.10: There are countless languages spoken across the world, and every single one carries meaning for its speakers.
14.11: But if I don't understand the language, I am a foreigner to the speaker—and the speaker is a foreigner to me. We are strangers to each other.
14.12: You are clearly eager for spiritual things—so channel that zeal toward gifts that build others up.
14.13: So if you speak in a tongue, pray also for the ability to interpret what you have said.
14.14: When I pray in a tongue, my spirit is active in prayer—but my mind produces nothing anyone can receive.
14.15: So here is what I will do: I will pray with my spirit fully engaged—and I will pray so that my mind is engaged too. I will sing praise with my spirit—and with my mind as well.
14.16: If you offer a blessing only in the Spirit, how can an outsider sitting with you say 'Amen' to your thanksgiving? They have no idea what you said.
14.17: Your heart may be sincere in giving thanks, but the person beside you receives nothing.
14.18: I am thankful to God—I speak in tongues more than any of you.
14.19: Yet in a gathered congregation I would rather say five words that people can understand than ten thousand words in a tongue. Five words that teach are worth more than a flood of untranslated speech.
14.20: Brothers and sisters, don't stay at the childhood level in your thinking. When it comes to evil, yes—be as innocent as infants. But in your understanding, grow up.
14.21: The scriptures themselves say it—and the Lord is the speaker—'With foreign tongues and the lips of strangers I will speak to this people, and even then they will not listen.' That word was a sign of judgment, not a blessing.
14.22: Tongues, then, are a sign—not for the community of faith, but for those who do not believe. Prophecy, by contrast, is not aimed at outsiders but at those who belong to God.
14.23: So imagine the whole congregation gathering and everyone speaking in tongues. Then an outsider or a nonbeliever walks in—won't they conclude that you have all lost your minds?
14.24: But if all are prophesying and a nonbeliever or outsider walks in—the word spoken to the congregation becomes the word that pierces that person. They find themselves exposed and called to account by what they hear.
14.25: The hidden things of their heart are laid bare. And what happens? They fall on their face in worship, declaring, 'God is truly in this place.'
14.26: So then, brothers and sisters, what should your gatherings look like? When you come together, one person may bring a song of praise, another a word of teaching, another a revelation, another a tongue, another an interpretation. Let everything serve the building up of the community.
14.27: When tongues are used, limit it to two or three at most, and have them speak one at a time. And someone must interpret.
14.28: If no one present can interpret, the tongue-speaker should stay silent in the assembly—using that gift in private prayer to God instead.
14.29: Let two or three prophets speak, and the rest of the congregation should carefully discern what has been said.
14.30: If a fresh word comes to someone else sitting in the congregation, the first speaker should give way.
14.31: You can all take turns prophesying, so that the whole community is taught and strengthened.
14.32: The prophetic impulse does not override a prophet's self-control—what the Spirit stirs within them remains under their own governance.
14.33: For the God we serve is not the author of chaos but of peace. This is the pattern in every congregation of God's people.
14.34: Women are to be silent in the assemblies; they are not permitted to speak, but are to hold their place, as the Law itself teaches.
14.35: If they have questions about what was said, let them ask their husbands at home. It is a dishonour to the assembly for a woman to speak up in that context.
14.36: Did the word of God begin with you? Did it reach you alone? Do you imagine yourselves the sole arbiters of how it is practiced?
14.37: Anyone who claims to be a prophet or a Spirit-gifted person should be able to recognize that what I am writing here carries the authority of the Lord.
14.38: If someone refuses to acknowledge it, let them remain in their refusal—God will sort it out.
14.39: So, my dear brothers and sisters: be zealous for prophecy, and don't shut down the gift of tongues.
14.40: Only let everything be done fittingly and with good order.

7.1: On the questions you raised in your letter — yes, you quoted back to me the principle that it is good for a man to abstain from marriage. I want to address that carefully.
7.2: But given the reality of sexual temptation in Corinth, the right path for most people is marriage: one man, one woman, each belonging fully to the other.
7.3: A husband owes his wife the intimacy of marriage — it is a debt, not a favor. And a wife owes the same to her husband.
7.4: In marriage the body becomes shared: neither spouse can treat their own body as private property to withhold at will. This cuts both ways — he has authority over her body, and she has authority over his.
7.5: Do not withhold from each other — except by joint, time-limited decision for a season of focused prayer. Then reunite. Otherwise you hand Satan an opening through your own vulnerability.
7.6: What I just said is pastoral accommodation to reality, not apostolic law. I am not commanding sexual abstinence in marriage; I am permitting temporary, mutual, purposeful restraint.
7.7: Personally I would prefer everyone to live as I do — but that wish collides with reality. God gives different gifts: what I have is one kind of grace-endowment; what a married person has is another. Neither is inferior.
7.8: My word to the single and the widowed: staying as you are, as I am, is a genuine good — not a lesser path, not a consolation prize.
7.9: But if remaining single proves to be a smoldering struggle rather than a peaceful gift, marry. Marriage is not defeat; it is wisdom. Burning unmet desire is the real danger.
7.10: For those already married, I relay a direct word from the Lord himself, not my own apostolic judgment: a wife must not leave her husband.
7.11: If she does leave, her only options are to remain single or to return to him — that door stays open, always. And equally, a husband must not initiate divorce against his wife.
7.12: On the following question, I give my own apostolic judgment — I have no direct word of Jesus to relay here. If a Christian man has an unbelieving wife who is content to stay in the marriage, he must not end it.
7.13: The same principle holds for a Christian woman with an unbelieving husband who is content to remain — let the marriage stand.
7.14: Here is the reason: the believer draws the whole household into the sphere of God's covenant. The unbelieving spouse inhabits sacred space. Your children prove this — you already treat them as belonging to God, not as outsiders to his reach.
7.15: But if the unbeliever walks out, release them — you are not bound by a marriage the other party has already abandoned. God's calling for your life is peace, not perpetual servitude to a broken bond.
7.16: And in any case, the future belongs to God, not you: you cannot know whether staying will win your spouse to faith — that uncertainty is precisely why peace, not burden, is the right frame for this whole question.
7.17: The governing principle, which I teach in every church I serve: live faithfully in the situation where God placed you when he called you. Your circumstances at conversion are your starting point, not your ceiling.
7.18: Were you Jewish when God called you? Don't try to erase that heritage. Were you Gentile? Don't submit to circumcision as though it changes your standing. The real issues lie elsewhere.
7.19: In the new order, circumcision is a non-issue and its absence is equally a non-issue. What defines faithfulness now is keeping God's commandments — the moral substance of the law, fulfilled in Christ.
7.20: Stay where God found you. The place and condition of your conversion is the ground from which your whole life with God grows.
7.21: Were you a slave when God called you? Don't let that define your horizon — you serve Christ now, and that transforms everything. But if freedom becomes possible, take it without hesitation.
7.22: Here is the great reversal: a slave who belongs to Christ is the Lord's own freed man — Christ has already liberated what no human owner can touch. And a free person who belongs to Christ is Christ's own servant — the most meaningful slavery imaginable.
7.23: The purchase price of your freedom was Christ's own blood. Do not sell that freedom back to human masters — to human approval, to social pressure, to the opinion of those who would define your worth.
7.24: Brothers, the place where God found you is the place where you can walk with him — stay there, in his presence, and let that transform the circumstances from within.
7.25: On the question of those who have never married — I have no direct word of Jesus to pass on here, only my own apostolic judgment. I offer it as someone who has received God's mercy and aims to be reliable.
7.26: Given the pressure of this present moment in history — we live at the hinge of the ages — staying as you are is the strategically wise course.
7.27: If you are married, don't engineer an exit. If you are single, don't let finding a spouse become your consuming preoccupation.
7.28: Marriage is no sin — if you marry, you are not disobeying God; the same for a woman who chooses to marry. But I want to be honest with you: married life brings its own pressures and burdens. It is those pressures I am trying to spare you, not the marriage itself.
7.29: Here is the urgent point, brothers: the clock is running down — the age is reaching its end. So live with your eyes on eternity, not absorbed in your circumstances. Married people should not be consumed by marriage —
7.30: grief should not be the last word on your life; joy should not be your ultimate foundation; possessions should sit loosely in your hands —
7.31: engage the world without being mastered by it. The whole present structure of things is dissolving; the age that replaces it is your real home.
7.32: What I desire for you is an undivided heart. The single man can give his whole attention to God's affairs — his one governing question is how to live for the Lord.
7.33: The married man's attention is necessarily split: he must think about his household, his finances, how to honor his wife —
7.34: — and that divided loyalty is the simple nature of married life, not a fault but a fact. The unmarried woman has the freedom to offer her whole self — body and spirit — to God's purposes without competing claims. The married woman must weave her devotion through the fabric of a shared life.
7.35: I am not laying down a law or trying to trap you in guilt. My one aim is to point you toward what is truly noble: an undistracted, undivided attentiveness to the Lord.
7.36: But if a man finds that his restraint is beginning to dishonor the woman he is pledged to — if time is passing and the tension is real and marriage is what must happen — let him act. That is not weakness; it is not sin; it is the right and honorable thing to do.
7.37: But if a man has made a settled, free decision — no external pressure, desire genuinely mastered, chosen from the heart — to maintain a celibate commitment with the woman he is pledged to, he does something genuinely good.
7.38: Marriage is good. Celibacy chosen freely, for the kingdom's sake, is better still. Both are genuine goods; neither is a mistake or a spiritual shortcut.
7.39: Marriage is a bond that holds for life — that is its very nature. Death releases the bond, and the widow is genuinely free to remarry — anyone she chooses. One qualification only: he must belong to the Lord.
7.40: But from where I stand — and I believe I am reading this by the Spirit of God — a widow who stays single chooses the better joy. That is my considered, Spirit-guided judgment.

8.1: On the matter of food sacrificed to idols — yes, we all have the relevant theological knowledge. But knowledge on its own inflates the knower; it is love that actually builds people up and strengthens the community.
8.2: The very confidence of 'I have this figured out' is a warning sign — whoever is certain they have grasped something has not yet understood how little they actually know.
8.3: What matters is not that you know but that you are known — and to love God and be loved by him is the only knowledge that ultimately secures you.
8.4: So when it comes to the practical question — can we eat what passed through a pagan temple? — the theology is correct: an idol is a fiction, a nothing dressed in stone and gold. There is only one God.
8.5: The world is thick with so-called gods and lords — the temples are real, the cult is real, the social pressure to participate is real —
8.6: — but we confess a different reality entirely. One God: the Father, source of everything that exists, the goal toward whom everything moves and into whom we are gathered. One Lord: Jesus the Messiah, through whom the whole creation came to be and through whom we ourselves have been remade. That is our creed, our identity, our world.
8.7: But this knowledge is not evenly distributed — and here is where everything changes. Some believers were deep in idol worship before they came to faith. When they eat food that passed through a pagan shrine, they cannot shake the feeling that they are participating in something spiritually real. Their conscience is still tender at that point, and eating violates something in them.
8.8: Let's be clear about what is not at stake here: food has zero power to alter your standing before God. Eating or abstaining changes nothing in your relationship with him.
8.9: The danger, though, is this: your theological freedom, exercised without thought for others, can become the very obstacle over which a fragile believer falls.
8.10: Picture it: you, the theologically confident one, reclining at a meal in an idol's precinct. A believer with a tender conscience sees you. Your example doesn't liberate them — it gives them permission to do something they still believe is wrong. You have weaponized your freedom against a weaker brother.
8.11: And so this person — the one whose faith is fragile, the one Jesus bled and died to save — is being destroyed by your sophisticated theology. Sit with that.
8.12: When you wound a fellow believer's conscience this carelessly, you are not simply being thoughtless. You are sinning against Christ himself, who is present and at stake in every one of his people.
8.13: My conclusion is non-negotiable: if a simple meal has the power to trip up someone for whom Christ died, then I will give up meat entirely — permanently, for life — rather than use my freedom as a weapon against a vulnerable fellow believer.

9.1: Four questions before I go further: Am I not a free man? Am I not a genuine apostle? Did I not see the risen Lord Jesus with my own eyes? And are you Corinthians not yourselves the living proof of my apostolic labor?
9.2: Others may dispute my apostleship — that argument belongs to them. But you, the Corinthian church, cannot dispute it: your own existence is the certificate stamped by God himself on my calling.
9.3: When critics put me on trial, this is what I put on the witness stand.
9.4: As an apostle, do I not have the basic right to be fed and sustained by the communities I serve?
9.5: Do I not have the right — as every other apostle does, as the Lord's own brothers do, as Peter does — to travel with a believing wife who shares in the work?
9.6: Are Barnabas and I alone in the apostolic circle expected to hold down secular employment to survive while we serve you?
9.7: Three illustrations from ordinary working life: no soldier pays for his own gear and rations — the army feeds its soldiers. No vine-grower forfeits the harvest of what they planted. No shepherd is denied milk from the flock they tend. Work earns its keep. That is simply how things work.
9.8: Am I reasoning from mere human common sense? The Torah itself says as much — this is not my invention.
9.9: Moses wrote: Do not muzzle the ox while it works the threshing floor. Do you think that regulation was God's primary concern — the welfare of livestock?
9.10: Of course not — this text was written for the sake of workers like us. The underlying principle is this: anyone who labors — the plowman breaking ground in autumn, the thresher processing grain in summer — works with the legitimate expectation of sharing in the results. The ox is an illustration; we are the application.
9.11: We have invested something immeasurable in you — the gospel, the Spirit's presence, eternal life itself. Is it so great a burden to ask that you meet our physical needs in return?
9.12: Other workers in Corinth exercise this very right over you — and we have a stronger claim than any of them. Yet we have never claimed it. We absorb every hardship, bear every deprivation, rather than let any legitimate right of ours become an obstacle to the gospel's free advance.
9.13: Consider how the temple system has always been structured: priests live from what is consecrated to the sanctuary; altar servants have an appointed share in the altar's portions. That provision is built into the very architecture of sacred service.
9.14: And the Lord Jesus himself established this same principle for gospel heralds: those who announce the good news are to be materially sustained by those who receive it. It is his command, not my preference.
9.15: Yet I have used none of these rights — deliberately, consistently, from the beginning. And I am not writing this argument in order to start claiming them now. I would sooner die penniless than let anyone strip away the one thing I have to glory in before God: that I offered the gospel free of charge.
9.16: The preaching itself is not my boast — I had no choice in the matter. A compulsion from above was laid upon me. If I refuse to preach, I fall under divine judgment. There is no glory in doing what you cannot escape doing.
9.17: If I preached purely by free personal choice, there would be wages due. But I preach under orders — and a steward who simply does what the master requires is fulfilling a duty, not earning a bonus.
9.18: So what is my boast, my genuine wage? This: to preach the gospel without ever charging for it. That voluntary renunciation of rights — that free, deliberate gift — is the one thing I do out of pure choice, and therefore the one thing I can call my own glory.
9.19: Here is the paradox I live inside: I am beholden to no one — free as a person can be — yet I have voluntarily enslaved myself to every person I encounter, because winning them for Christ matters more than preserving my freedom.
9.20: Among Jews I live as a Jew — not as performance but as genuine solidarity with their world — in order to bring them to the Messiah. Among Torah-observant believers I live within the Torah framework — though I am no longer bound by it as a covenant requirement — to reach those who are.
9.21: Among Gentiles I adopt Gentile ways — their food, their customs, their social world — but let no one misread that as lawlessness toward God. I am under the law of the Messiah, which fulfills and transcends the Torah. This is service, not license.
9.22: To those whose faith is fragile, I identify with their fragility rather than brandishing my theological freedom at them. I make myself adaptable to everyone I meet — not out of people-pleasing, but because every soul I can reach through Christ is worth the sacrifice of my personal preferences.
9.23: Everything I do — the flexibility, the self-denial, the voluntary weakness — is for the gospel. I want to be a full participant in what it promises, not merely a delivery agent who hands good news to others and never enters it himself.
9.24: You know how a race works: every runner takes the field, but only one crosses the finish first and takes the wreath. Let that be the image for your whole life in Christ — run as though winning actually matters, because it does.
9.25: Every serious athlete disciplines every appetite — diet, sleep, training schedules — for the sake of a laurel wreath that wilts within the week. We are in training for something that will never decay, never fade, never be taken away.
9.26: My life is not an aimless sprint — I know exactly where the finish line is. And I am not shadowboxing — I land actual blows on an actual opponent, my own flesh.
9.27: I batter my own body into submission — the appetites, the comforts, the instinct for self-preservation — because the race demands it. My greatest fear is not criticism from others. It is this: that I would spend my whole life calling others to the finish line and be disqualified from crossing it myself.

15.1: Brothers and sisters, let me remind you of the Good News I preached to you. You welcomed it then, and you still stand on it today.
15.2: It is this message that saves you—but only if you keep holding to it as I proclaimed it. Otherwise your faith has been for nothing.
15.3: What I passed on to you was what had first been handed on to me—the very heart of the message: that Christ died for our sins, exactly as the Scriptures foretold.
15.4: He was buried, and then on the third day he rose from the dead, just as the Scriptures said he would.
15.5: After that he appeared to Peter and then to the rest of the twelve disciples.
15.6: After that he appeared at one time to more than five hundred of his followers, most of whom are still alive—though some have since died.
15.7: Then he appeared to James and later to all the apostles.
15.8: Last of all, as though to someone born at the wrong time, he also appeared to me.
15.9: For I am the least deserving of all the apostles. I don't even deserve to be called an apostle, because I persecuted God's church.
15.10: Yet whatever I am now, it is all because God poured out his special favor on me—and not without result. I have worked harder than any of the other apostles; though it was not I, but God's grace working through me.
15.11: So whether I preached to you or they did, the message is the same—and you believed it.
15.12: But tell me this—since we preach that Christ rose from the dead, why are some of you saying there will be no resurrection of the dead?
15.13: For if there is no resurrection of the dead, then Christ has not been raised either.
15.14: And if Christ has not been raised, then all our preaching is pointless, and your faith is pointless as well.
15.15: And we apostles would all be lying about God—for we have said that God raised Christ from the dead. That cannot be true if there is no resurrection of the dead.
15.16: And if there is no resurrection of the dead, then Christ has not been raised.
15.17: And if Christ has not been raised, then your faith is useless and you are still guilty of your sins.
15.18: In that case, all who have died believing in Christ are utterly lost.
15.19: And if our hope in Christ is only for this life, we are more to be pitied than anyone in the world.
15.20: But in fact, Christ has been raised from the dead. He is the first of the great harvest of all who have died.
15.21: Just as death came into the world through a man, now the resurrection from the dead has begun through another man.
15.22: Just as everyone dies because we all belong to Adam, everyone who belongs to Christ will be given new life.
15.23: But this will happen in the right order: Christ was raised as the first of the harvest; then all who belong to Christ will be raised when he returns.
15.24: After that the end will come, when he will turn the Kingdom over to God the Father, having destroyed every ruler and authority and power.
15.25: For Christ must reign until he humbles all his enemies beneath his feet.
15.26: And the last enemy to be destroyed is death itself.
15.27: The Scripture says, 'God has put all things under his authority.' Of course, when it says 'all things are under his authority,' that does not include God himself, who gave Christ his authority over all things.
15.28: Then, when all things are under his authority, the Son will put himself under God's authority, so that God, who gave his Son authority over all things, will be utterly supreme over everything everywhere.
15.29: If the dead will not be raised at all, what point is there in people being baptized on behalf of those who have died? Why do it at all?
15.30: And why should we ourselves risk our lives hour by hour?
15.31: For I swear, dear brothers and sisters, that I face death daily. This is as certain as my pride in what Christ Jesus our Lord has done in you.
15.32: And what value was there in fighting those ferocious opponents in Ephesus—if there will be no resurrection from the dead? If there is no resurrection, then as the Scripture says, 'Let's feast and drink, for tomorrow we die!'
15.33: Don't be fooled by those who say such things, for 'bad company corrupts good character.'
15.34: Think clearly about what is right and stop sinning. For to your shame I say that some of you have no knowledge of God at all.
15.35: But someone may ask, 'How will the dead be raised? What kind of bodies will they have?'
15.36: What a foolish question! When you put a seed into the ground, it doesn't grow into a plant unless it dies first.
15.37: And what you put in the ground is not the plant that will grow, but only a bare seed of wheat or whatever you are planting.
15.38: Then God gives it the new body he wants it to have. A different plant grows from each kind of seed.
15.39: Similarly there are different kinds of flesh—one kind for humans, another for animals, another for birds, and another for fish.
15.40: There are also bodies in the heavens and bodies on the earth. The glory of the heavenly bodies is different from the beauty of the earthly bodies.
15.41: The sun has one kind of glory, while the moon shines with a different glory, and the stars shine with yet another. Even the stars differ from each other in their beauty and brilliance.
15.42: It is the same way with the resurrection of the dead. Our earthly bodies are planted in the ground when we die, but they will be raised to live forever.
15.43: Our bodies are buried in brokenness, but they will be raised in glory. They are buried in weakness, but they will be raised in strength.
15.44: They are buried as natural, earthly bodies, but they will be raised as spiritual bodies. For just as there are natural bodies, there are also spiritual bodies.
15.45: The Scriptures tell us, 'The first man, Adam, became a living person.' But the last Adam—that is, Christ—is a life-giving Spirit.
15.46: What comes first is the natural body, then the spiritual body comes later.
15.47: Adam, the first man, was made from the dust of the earth, while Christ, the second man, came from heaven.
15.48: Earthly people are like the earthly man, and heavenly people are like the heavenly man.
15.49: Just as we are now like the earthly man, we will someday be like the heavenly man.
15.50: What I am saying, dear brothers and sisters, is that our physical bodies cannot inherit the Kingdom of God. These dying bodies cannot inherit what will last forever.
15.51: But let me reveal to you a wonderful secret. We will not all die, but we will all be transformed!
15.52: It will happen in a moment, in the blink of an eye, when the last trumpet is blown. For when the trumpet sounds, those who have died will be raised to live forever, and we who are living will also be transformed.
15.53: For our dying bodies must be transformed into bodies that will never die; our mortal bodies must be transformed into immortal bodies.
15.54: Then, when our dying bodies have been transformed into bodies that will never die, this Scripture will be fulfilled: 'Death is swallowed up in victory.'
15.55: 'O death, where is your victory? O death, where is your sting?'
15.56: For sin is the sting that results in death, and the law gives sin its power.
15.57: But thank God! He gives us victory over sin and death through our Lord Jesus Christ.
15.58: So, my dear brothers and sisters, be strong and immovable. Always work enthusiastically for the Lord, for you know that nothing you do for the Lord is ever useless.

16.1: Now regarding the offering being collected for God's people in Jerusalem. You should follow the same procedure I gave to the churches in Galatia.
16.2: On the first day of each week, you should each put aside a portion of the money you have earned, saving it up. Don't wait until I arrive and then try to collect it all at once.
16.3: When I come, I will write letters of recommendation for the messengers you choose to carry your gift to Jerusalem.
16.4: If it seems appropriate for me to go along, they can travel with me.
16.5: I am coming to visit you after I have been to Macedonia, for I am planning to travel through Macedonia first.
16.6: I may stay there the rest of the winter so you can send me on my way to wherever I go next.
16.7: I don't want to stop by for just a short visit on my way through. I want to come and stay awhile, if the Lord will let me.
16.8: In the meantime, I will be staying here at Ephesus until the Festival of Pentecost.
16.9: There is a wide-open door here for a great work, even though many oppose me.
16.10: If Timothy comes, make him feel welcome and at ease, for he is doing the Lord's work, just as I am.
16.11: Don't let anyone treat him with contempt. Send him on his way with your blessing when he returns to me. I am looking forward to seeing him soon, along with the other believers.
16.12: Now about our brother Apollos—I urged him strongly to visit you with the other believers, but he was not willing to come right now. He will see you later when he has the opportunity.
16.13: Be on guard. Stand firm in the faith. Be courageous. Be strong.
16.15: You know that Stephanas and his household were the first of the believers in Greece, and they have devoted themselves to serving God's people. Dear brothers and sisters, I urge you
16.16: to honor the leadership of everyone like them, and of all who work and labor alongside them.
16.17: I am very glad that Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus have come here. They have been making up for the support you were not able to give me.
16.18: They have been a wonderful encouragement to me, as they have been to you. You must show your appreciation to all who serve like this.
16.19: The churches here in the province of Asia send greetings in the Lord, as do our brother and sister Aquila and Prisca, along with the church that meets in their home.
16.20: All the brothers and sisters here send their greetings. Greet each other with a holy kiss.
16.21: Here is my greeting in my own handwriting—Paul.
16.22: If anyone does not love the Lord, a curse be on them. Our Lord, come!
16.23: May the grace of the Lord Jesus be with you.
16.24: My love to all of you in Christ Jesus.

10.1: Brothers, I want you to take this to heart: our ancestors all marched under the cloud and all walked through the sea,
10.2: and in the cloud and in the sea they were all, in effect, baptized into Moses—united to him as their deliverer.
10.3: They all ate the same Spirit-given food
10.4: and drank the same Spirit-given drink—for they were drinking from a spiritual Rock that traveled with them, and that Rock was Christ himself.
10.5: Yet God was not pleased with most of them—their bodies were strewn across the wilderness.
10.6: These events stand as warnings for us, so we won't fall into the same craving for evil that destroyed them.
10.7: Don't become idolaters as some of them did. The scripture says: 'The people sat down to eat and drink, then got up to revel.'
10.8: Don't commit sexual immorality the way some of them did—twenty-three thousand died in a single day.
10.9: Don't put Christ to the test as some of them did—and were killed by serpents.
10.10: Don't grumble, as some of them grumbled—they were cut down by the destroying angel.
10.11: All of this befell them as a warning, and it was recorded for our sake—we who stand at the very end of the age.
10.12: So if you think you're standing secure, watch out—the fall can come quickly.
10.13: Every temptation you face is the ordinary kind that all human beings face. God is faithful—he will not allow you to be tested beyond your strength, and when the test comes, he will open a way through so that you can hold on.
10.14: This is why, dear friends, you must run from idol worship.
10.15: I'm addressing you as thinking people—weigh what I say.
10.16: When we take the cup of blessing and give thanks over it, are we not sharing in the blood of Christ? When we break the bread, are we not sharing in the body of Christ?
10.17: There is one loaf, and because we all share it, we who are many form one body.
10.18: Think about Israel as they offered sacrifices—those who ate from the offering became participants at the altar itself.
10.19: What am I getting at? That meat offered to an idol has any real significance? Or that an idol itself amounts to anything?
10.20: No—what I mean is this: the sacrifices that pagans make are offered to demons, not to God. And I refuse to see you become partners with demons.
10.21: You cannot drink from the Lord's cup and from the cup of demons at the same time. You cannot sit at the Lord's table and at the table of demons.
10.22: Are we really trying to make the Lord jealous? Do we imagine we're stronger than he is?
10.23: 'Everything is allowed'—but not everything is good. 'Everything is allowed'—but not everything strengthens others.
10.24: Stop pursuing your own advantage—pursue the advantage of the person next to you.
10.25: Buy and eat whatever is in the market without making it a matter of conscience—
10.26: since 'the earth belongs to the Lord, and everything it contains.'
10.27: If a pagan invites you to dinner and you choose to accept, eat whatever they serve without making an issue of conscience.
10.28: But if someone tells you, 'This meat was offered to an idol,' don't eat it—both to protect the conscience of the person who said it and to honor what conscience requires.
10.29: I mean his conscience, not yours. But you might ask: why should my freedom be limited by another person's scruples?
10.30: If I eat with genuine thanksgiving, why should I be condemned for food I received with gratitude?
10.31: The answer is this: whether you're eating, drinking, or doing anything else—make it all about the glory of God.
10.32: Don't become a stumbling block to anyone—not to Jewish people, not to Gentiles, not to the church of God.
10.33: Take my lead: I try to accommodate everyone in every situation—not for my own benefit, but for the benefit of as many people as possible, so that they might be saved.

11.1: Imitate me—the way I imitate Christ.
11.2: I'm glad to commend you for remembering me in all this and for holding to the teaching I passed on to you.
11.3: Here is something I want you to understand: the relational head of every man is Christ, the relational head of a wife is her husband, and the relational head of Christ is God—a pattern of ordered love from the Trinity downward.
11.4: Any man who prays or prophesies with his head covered shames his head—Christ.
11.5: But any woman who prays or prophesies with her head uncovered shames her head—her husband. It's equivalent to having her head shaved.
11.6: If a woman won't cover her head, she may as well cut her hair off—but since cropping or shaving a woman's hair is disgraceful, she should keep her head covered.
11.7: A man should not cover his head, because he is the image and reflected glory of God; but woman is the reflected glory of man.
11.8: Man did not originate from woman, but woman from man—
11.9: and man was not made for woman's sake, but woman for the sake of man.
11.10: For this reason—and because of the watching angels—a woman should have authority over her own head.
11.11: But in the Lord, woman and man are not independent of each other—
11.12: for as woman came from man, so now every man enters the world through woman—and all of it comes from God.
11.13: Use your own judgment: is it right for a woman to pray to God with her head uncovered?
11.14: Doesn't the natural order itself instruct you? Long hair on a man is a mark of dishonor—
11.15: but for a woman, her long hair is her glory. Her hair has been given to her as a natural covering.
11.16: If anyone wants to argue the point—we have no other custom, and neither do the churches of God.
11.17: But what I'm about to address next I cannot commend: your gatherings are doing you more harm than good.
11.18: To begin with, I'm told that when you assemble as a congregation there are factions forming—and I'm inclined to believe it.
11.19: Such divisions may be inevitable—they expose who among you is genuinely tested and approved.
11.20: When you gather like this, it simply isn't the Lord's Supper that you're eating—
11.21: because each of you rushes ahead with your own meal, and one person goes hungry while another has too much to drink.
11.22: Don't you have homes to eat and drink in? Or is your aim to shame the church of God and humiliate the poor among you? What can I say? Should I praise you? Not for this.
11.23: What I'm about to say I received directly from the Lord, and I passed it on to you: On the night he was handed over, the Lord Jesus took bread,
11.24: and after giving thanks he broke it and said, 'This is my body, given for you. Keep doing this to remember me.'
11.25: He did the same with the cup after the meal: 'This cup is the new covenant, sealed in my blood. Every time you drink it, do it to remember me.'
11.26: Every time you eat this bread and drink this cup, you are announcing the Lord's death—and you will keep doing so until he returns.
11.27: This means that anyone who eats the bread or drinks the cup carelessly is answerable for the body and blood of the Lord himself.
11.28: Let everyone examine themselves before they eat and drink—then eat.
11.29: Eating and drinking without recognizing the Lord's body means eating and drinking a verdict against yourself.
11.30: This is why many of you are weak and ill, and some have died.
11.31: If we honestly judged ourselves, we would not face this kind of judgment from God.
11.32: When the Lord brings this discipline on us, it is to keep us from being swept up in the world's final condemnation.
11.33: So then, brothers and sisters, when you come together to share the meal, wait for one another.
11.34: If you're hungry before you arrive, eat at home—so that your gatherings don't bring judgment down on you. The remaining questions I'll deal with in person when I come.

12.1: I don't want you in the dark about Spirit-given gifts, brothers and sisters.
12.2: You know what you were before: pagans, swept along—however you were led—toward speechless idols.
12.3: So here is how you discern: no one speaking by God's Spirit calls Jesus accursed—and no one can confess 'Jesus is Lord' except by the Holy Spirit.
12.4: The gifts are varied, but they all come from the same Spirit.
12.5: The forms of service are varied, but it is the same Lord they serve.
12.6: The activities are varied, but the same God is at work in all of them through everyone.
12.7: Each person is given a visible expression of the Spirit's presence—and it is for the benefit of everyone.
12.8: Through the Spirit, one person receives a word of wisdom; another, through the same Spirit, a word of knowledge.
12.9: Another receives Spirit-given trust; another, gifts of healing—all from the same one Spirit.
12.10: Another receives the power to work miracles; another, prophecy; another, the ability to discern spirits; another, various kinds of tongues; and another, the interpretation of those tongues.
12.11: All of these are the work of one and the same Spirit, who gives to each person exactly what he chooses.
12.12: Think of the human body: one body, many parts—yet all those many parts together form one body. That is exactly how it is with Christ.
12.13: In one Spirit we were all baptized into one body—Jewish or Greek, slave or free—and we were all immersed in the one Spirit, the same Spirit given to each of us.
12.14: A body is not a single part—it's made of many.
12.15: Suppose the foot were to say, 'Since I'm not a hand, I don't belong to the body'—that declaration wouldn't make it any less part of the body.
12.16: Or if the ear were to say, 'Since I'm not an eye, I don't belong to the body'—that declaration wouldn't make it any less a part of the body.
12.17: If the whole body were only an eye, how would you hear? If it were only an ear, how would you smell anything?
12.18: As it is, God has arranged each and every part of the body exactly as he saw fit.
12.19: If everything were just one part, there would be no body at all.
12.20: But there are many parts—and they together make one body.
12.21: The eye can't tell the hand, 'I don't need you.' And the head can't say to the feet, 'I don't need you.'
12.22: In fact, the parts that seem weakest are often the most essential—
12.23: and we give extra care and honor to the parts we think look less distinguished, while our private parts are covered with the greatest propriety—
12.24: things our more presentable parts don't require. God himself has arranged the body this way, giving extra honor to whatever seemed to lack it—
12.25: so that there would be no split in the body, and every part would genuinely care for every other part.
12.26: When one part of the body suffers, all the other parts suffer alongside it. When one part is honored, all the others share in the joy.
12.27: You are the body of Christ—and each of you is a distinct, irreplaceable part of it.
12.28: And God himself has appointed roles within the church: first apostles, second prophets, third teachers—then those who work miracles, those who have gifts of healing, those who help others, those who lead, those who speak in various kinds of tongues.
12.29: Is everyone an apostle? Is everyone a prophet? Is everyone a teacher? Does everyone work miracles?
12.30: Does everyone have gifts of healing? Does everyone speak in tongues? Does everyone interpret?
12.31: Pursue the greater gifts with eagerness—but I am going to show you a way that surpasses them all.
